coredata.dc:title: Heterogeneous interface of MnFeSe electrocatalyst for high-performance overall water splitting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Wenzhi
Guo, Mengmeng
Chai, Dong-Feng
Dong, Guohua
Bai, Liming
Zhang, Zhuanfang
Zhang, Xinjia
Guo, Dongxuan
coredata.dc:description: Heterogeneous structures with rich phase boundaries are attractive for surface-mediated catalyst applications. Therefore, a practical solution involves constructing heterogeneous materials to maximize the functionality of electrocatalysts. In this study, we assembled a transition metal-based manganese-ferro selenide heterostructure nanosheet, combined with boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) on a foam nickel self-supporting electrocatalyst, using a two-step pyrolysis method (B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF). The interface between the MnSe and FeSe components of the heterogeneous catalyst modulates the electronic structure, facilitates enhanced charge transfer, and improves structural stability, thereby increasing the efficiency of electrocatalytic water splitting. Consequently, the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF heterostructure reduces the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) overpotential to 89 mV and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) overpotential to 197 mV. Moreover, this catalyst requires only 1.52 V for the water splitting voltage at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. This work provides new insights into enhancing the intrinsic activity of overall water-splitting systems with heterogeneous structures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Overall water splitting
Electrocatalyst
Heterostructure
Transition element
Selenide
Dual-functional

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the formation of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF (a), SEM image of B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF (b), SEM image of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF (c), TEM image of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF (d), HR-TEM image of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF (e), SAED image of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF (f), EDS elemental mapping images (g).

The structure characteristics of Mn3O4, B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF and B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF sample: XRD patterns (a), N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (b), Pore diameter diagram (c), FT-IR spectra (d), Image of nickel foam after water droplet (e). Image of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF after water droplet (f).
XPS spectra of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF sample: Survey spectrum (a), High-resolution of Mn 2p (b), Fe 2p (c), Se 3d (d), C 1s (e) and B 1s (f).

OER performance of Mn3O4, MnFeO/NF, B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF, B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF catalysts. Polarization curves (a), Comparison diagram of overpotential values (b), Tafel slope (c), TOF curves (d), Nyquist plots (e), Cdl value (f), Polarization curves for B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF before and after 5000 cycles of CV scanning (g), CP test of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF catalyst for 100-h at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 (h).

HER performance of Mn3O4, MnFeO/NF, B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF, B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF catalysts. Polarization curves (a), Comparison diagram of overpotential values (b), Tafel slope (c), TOF curves (d), Nyquist plots (e), Cdl value (f), Polarization curves for B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF before and after 5000 cycles of CV scanning (g), CP test of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF catalyst for 100-h at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 (h).

Faraday efficiency test device of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF||B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF (a and a1-a6), Faraday efficiency (b), The polarization curves of the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF||B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF and NF||NF at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 (c), The long-term stability of the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF||B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF (d), The potential comparison of Mn-based electrocatalysts (e).

head.ce:title: Heterogeneous interface of MnFeSe electrocatalyst for high-performance overall water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Qiqihar University, Qiqihar, 161006, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heterogeneous structures with rich phase boundaries are attractive for surface-mediated catalyst applications. Therefore, a practical solution involves constructing heterogeneous materials to maximize the functionality of electrocatalysts. In this study, we assembled a transition metal-based manganese-ferro selenide heterostructure nanosheet, combined with boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) on a foam nickel self-supporting electrocatalyst, using a two-step pyrolysis method (B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF). The interface between the MnSe and FeSe components of the heterogeneous catalyst modulates the electronic structure, facilitates enhanced charge transfer, and improves structural stability, thereby increasing the efficiency of electrocatalytic water splitting. Consequently, the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF heterostructure reduces the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) overpotential to 89 mV and the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) overpotential to 197 mV. Moreover, this catalyst requires only 1.52 V for the water splitting voltage at a current density of 10 mA cm−2. This work provides new insights into enhancing the intrinsic activity of overall water-splitting systems with heterogeneous structures.

The novel B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF catalyst was prepared using the solvothermal method, exhibiting excellent catalytic activity and stability in the overall water splitting reaction.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: With the progression of scientific and technological innovation and the exponential expansion of the global economy, the conflict between the utilization of fossil fuels and the necessity for environmental conservation has intensified the focus on electrochemical water splitting [1]. Electrocatalytic water splitting as a viable method for harnessing high-quality hydrogen fuel, which includes the Hydrogen Evolution Reaction (HER) and the Oxygen Evolution Reaction (OER), represents an exceptionally efficient, recyclable, and environmentally friendly protocol for hydrogen production [2]. Nevertheless, its energy efficiency is significantly compromised due to the high inherent energy barriers [3]. The sluggish kinetics associated with the multi-step electron transfer process, particularly for the OER, constrain the efficacy of the water splitting reaction. Consequently, the development of efficient electrocatalysts is imperative to overcome the formidable challenges posed by the prohibitively high activation energy barrier [4]. To date, platinum (Pt) and ruthenium (Ru)/iridium (Ir)-based materials have been identified as the most effective catalysts for HER and OER. However, their high cost and limited abundance have hindered widespread application [5]. Consequently, persistent efforts are essential for the development of electrocatalysts that are both cost-efficient and highly stable.

Current solutions primarily focus on element doping, introducing defects, and constructing heterogeneous structures in three-dimensional transition metal oxides (including Fe, Co, Ni, and Mn). Among these strategies, constructing heterogeneous structures is the most widely employed approach for modulating electrocatalytic water splitting. Generally, the interface of a heterogeneous catalyst serves as the direct site for reactions, enhancing the adsorption and desorption capacities of critical intermediates, providing a greater number of accessible active sites, modulating the electronic structure, and facilitating enhanced charge transfer, which improve the efficiency of electrocatalytic water splitting. For instance, Lin [6] using Ni5P4 microballs as the crystal seed, a novel Co/NiCoP nanoball (NSs) heterojunction was synthesized via the crystal seed template conversion method. Effective oxygen atom interface editing increased the oxidation state of the Co atoms, extended the Co–P bond length in the Co/NiCoP NSs heterojunction, thereby enhancing electron localization at the P site. This led to a significant improvement in both the HER and OER performance. Gu [7] et al. amalgamated phosphorus (P) and selenium (Se) atoms into an open-structured Co–Fe-based Prussian blue analogue through a single-step thermal decomposition approach, resulting in a heterostructure composed of CoPSe and FePSe3. The heterogeneous structures formed by the intersection of different crystal faces can provide abundant electrocatalytic active sites for HER/OER, promoting the reaction kinetics of HER/OER and thereby reducing the overpotential. Shivakumar P [8] has synthesized an ordered NiFeLDH/RuSe2 heterostructure for electrochemical water splitting reactions. The formation of the heterojunction enhances the active sites for adsorbing H+ and OH− ions, thereby achieving improved OER and HER performance. Yang [9] et al. had successfully fabricated a novel spherical flower-like MnO2/Co3(PO4)2 electrocatalyst via a straightforward one-step electrodeposition technique on nickel foam. This demonstrates the features of a p-p type heterojunction. It has been demonstrated that the establishment of heterogeneous structures supplies an efficacious strategy for modulating electrocatalytic water splitting. Heterogeneous structures featuring electronic interactions between distinct phases can augment the rate of electron transfer, modulate the electronic structure, and enhance the adsorption and desorption capabilities of critical intermediates, ultimately increasing the efficiency of electrocatalytic water splitting [10].

Based on this, boron-doped carbon quantum dots (B-CQDs) are prepared using a one-step calcination technique to enhance conductivity. Subsequently, B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF is directly synthesized on a foam nickel substrate using a simple solvent thermal method. As a result, B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF demonstrates exceptional dual-functional catalytic performance for both HER and OER in alkaline electrolytes, owing to its unique nanosheet structure and highly hydrophilic surface, which facilitate close contact between the active components and the electrolyte, thereby enhancing catalytic efficiency. Meanwhile, the unique MnSe and FeSe heterostructure facilitates more efficient charge transport pathways, promoting the rate of electron transfer, while the interface provides abundant active sites between the reactive constituents and the electrolyte. Furthermore, the presence of B-CQDs significantly enhances the rate of electron transfer in the compound. The oxygen-containing functional groups present on the surface of B-CQDs can effectively bind with metal ions owning to empty d orbitals, thereby forming stable composite materials enduring during HER and OER. Additionally, the robust adherence between B-CQDs/MnFeSe and the nickel foam substrate establishes an efficient conduit for gas expulsion and electron transfer [11,12]. Eventually, when the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF is integrated with an alkaline battery, it operates at a mere 1.52 V (10 mA cm−2) for comprehensive water splitting, showcasing remarkable long-term stability. Overall, this study offers a novel approach to the development and production of efficient dual-function electrocatalysts.

All chemicals employed are of analytical grade and require no further purification. Throughout the experiment, deionized water was utilized to prepare solutions and standard samples. Phloroglucinol (C6H6O3·2H2O), boric acid (H3BO3), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), ferric nitrate (FeNO3), selenium powder (Se), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were sourced from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Factory, Tianjin Tianli Chemical Reagent Limited Company, Harbin Reagent Chemical Factory, and Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagent Limited Company, Tianjin KeTong Chemical Reagent Limited Company, and Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory.

The foam nickel should be cut to dimensions of 2 × 4 cm placed within a beaker. After that, add acetone and subject it to ultrasonic treatment for 15 min to remove surface oils. Pour out the acetone and add 2 M hydrochloric acid solution, then subject the mixture to sonication for a duration of 15 min to eliminate the oxide layer adhering to its surface. Subsequently, the samples were subjected to ultrasonic disruption for a duration of 15 min in deionized water, ethanol, and deionized water successively, to eliminate any adsorbed ions from their surfaces. Subsequently, the processed nickel foam was dehydrated overnight at a temperature of 60 °C under vacuum conditions for subsequent utilization.

A mixture of phloroglucinol and boric acid, in a 1:1 ratio, is prepared and placed within a muffle furnace. It is then heated at a rate of 5 °C per minute until it reaches a temperature of 200 °C, maintained for a duration of 2 h. This process results in the formation of a red powder, which is identified as B-CQDs.

Prepare a solution by dissolving 0.6321 g of potassium permanganate and 0.3869 g of iron (III) nitrate in 20 mL of deionized water. Under magnetic stirring, introduce a 10 mL solution containing 0.0500 g of B-CQDs dropwise into the mixture. Once thoroughly mixed, transfer the resulting solution to a 40 mL capacity autoclave lined with Teflon-coated. Subsequently, the pretreated NF was ensconced within a high-pressure autoclave, where it was subjected to a temperature of 180 °C for 3 h within an electric hot-air drying oven. Thereafter, the resultant B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF was subjected to multiple rinses with deionized water and desiccated at a temperature of 60 °C for a period of 10 h before its utilization.

1.0720 g of selenium powder and 0.3869 g of sodium hydroxide were dissolved in 160 mL of deionized water. Subsequently, the solution was transferred to a 200 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene. After a 12 h incubation at 180 °C, a solution of sodium selenate is obtained. Prepared B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF were immersed in a 40 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel high-pressure reaction vessel 30 mL sodium selenate solution at 120 °C for 8 h under sealed conditions, resulting in the formation of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF.

The emission wavelength of carbon dots was characterized using a luminescence spectrometer. The microstructure and morphology of the prepared samples were meticulously examined utilizing a scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM). The elemental species and composition of the samples were exhaustively analyzed through energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images and SAED (Selected Area Electron Diffraction) were used to determine the phase composition, structural defects and crystal structure. The phase composition and lattice parameters of the electrode materials were meticulously evaluated via X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD). Furthermore, surface element information was accurately detected through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The determination of nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms at 77 K was achieved through N2 adsorption-desorption. The specific surface area and total pore volume of the catalyst were ascertained using BET and BJH methods, respectively. The dimensions and regularity of pore size were analyzed via the pore size distribution curve. The surface properties of the product were examined through Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR).

As depicted in FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1a), the peak at 3400, 1620, 1330, 1160 and 1050 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the –OH, C=C, B–O, C–O and C–B on carbon dots, which confirming the successful synthesis of boron-doped carbon dots [13–17]. Furthermore, the functional groups of carbon dots interact with the vacant d-orbitals of metal ions, which endows the catalyst with high durability for HER and OER [18–20]. In Fig. S1b, the TEM image of B-CQDs demonstrates that they are well-monodisperse microspheres with a diameter of approximately 10 nm. Undoubtedly, photoluminescence is typically an essential intrinsic attribute of CQDs, primarily due to the small size effect of B-CQDs. As illustrated in Fig. S1c, B-CQDs display a considerable fluorescence emission peak at 558 nm [21] when the excitation wavelength is 461 nm. Furthermore, it can be clearly observed that the color of B-CQDs aqueous solution changes from orange to light blue when sunlight was turn to UV–vis light (365 nm) in Fig. S1c inset.

Schematic illustration for the formation of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF as shown in Fig. 1 a. Fig. 1b and c displays SEM images of B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF and B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF, respectively. The uniform and dispersed small-size nanosheets provide abundant active sites and it is more conducive to the improvement of electrochemical performance [22]. Additionally, the morphology of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF has no obvious change after selenization, which indicates it would exhibit potential stability during electrocatalytic reaction process. Fig. 1d presents the TEM image of B- CQDs/MnFeSe/NF, which is coincidence with the SEM image. HR-TEM and SAED were employed to delve the internal heterostructure of MnSe and FeSe, as depicted in Fig. 1e. The HR-TEM images revealed the clear interface between MnSe and FeSe. And the lattice fringes are also distinctly visible with crystal plane spacings of 0.311 nm and 0.246 nm, corresponding to the (111) and (111) planes of MnSe and FeSe, respectively, which are consistent with their respective standard cards. Additionally, the corresponding SAED pattern corroborated the polymorphism of the nanosheets, as illustrated in Fig. 1f. In this figure, two well-ordered diffraction rings correspond to the (422) and (112) crystal faces of MnSe and FeSe, respectively. By utilizing ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis DRS) and analyzing the corresponding Tauc plots (Figs. S2a and b), it is found that MnSe/FeSe exhibits the strongest light absorption capability with a bandgap of 1.75 eV, further confirming its excellent electronic transport properties. Additionally, the positive slope in the Mott-Schottky plot indicates that MnSe/FeSe possesses n-type heterojunction characteristics (Figs. S2c and d). The valence band (EVB) values of MnSe and FeSe are calculated to be 0.63 eV and 2.22 eV, respectively, based on their conduction band (ECB) values determined from the intersection with the x-axis, along with the bandgap energies (Eg) shown in the figure using the relation E
g
= E
VB
- E
CB . This suggests that a significant charge separation effect occurs at the interface between MnSe and FeSe in this heterojunction, which effectively reduces electron-hole recombination and enhances catalytic efficiency. The element mapping presented in Fig. 1g demonstrates that B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF are obtained and all the elements of Mn, Fe, Se, O, C, and B are uniformly distributed. Meanwhile, EDS line spectra and atomic weight percentage are shown in Fig. S3.

The phase structure, functional groups and pore diameter and volume information B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF, B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF, and Mn3O4 were elucidated through XRD, FT-IR and N2 adsorption/desorption measurements. As expected in Fig. 2 a that the primary diffraction peaks of B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF are ascribed to Mn3O4 (PDF#24–0734) and Fe2O3 (PDF#72–0469), while the pronounced diffraction peaks of the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF electrocatalyst corroborate the presence of MnSe (PDF#27–0311) and FeSe (PDF#03–0533). As illustrated in Fig. 2b displays the isotherm curve of N2 adsorption-desorption, exhibiting the typical features of type IV isotherms. According to the IUPAC classification, this curve possesses an H3 hysteresis ring, suggesting the presence of mesopores within the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF composites [27,28]. And the specific surface area of the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF (243.0680 m2 g−1) is greater than Mn3O4 (48.1780 m2 g−1), and is similar with B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF (242.8560 m2 g−1). As given in Fig. 2c, the pore diameter distribution of the catalyst demonstrates that the pore diameter of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF (24.4500 nm) is notably higher than Mn3O4 (3.4150 nm), and B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF (3.8230 nm). The presence of a large pore diameter and mesoporous structure can enhance the BET specific surface area of the catalyst, offering a substantial number of active sites and then enhancing the dual-function activity of OER and HER. Fig. 2d, the peak at 523 cm−1 [23] corresponds to the Mn–O stretching vibration, while the broad peak at 3400 cm−1 24 is attributed to the O–H stretching vibration. B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF is associated with the Fe–O, B–O and C–B stretching vibration at 580, 1330 and 1050 cm−1 [24–26] Additionally, Mn–Se stretching vibration at 490 cm−1 [26] indicate B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF was acquired successfully. The consistency in peak shapes and positions across each catalyst in the figure suggests that the original Mn3O4 crystal structure complying with the catalyst remains unchanged following the doping modification and selenization process.

Improving the hydrophilia for the catalyst is critical to enhance the electrocatalytic effect. To assess the surface properties of the catalyst [34], a measurement of the water contact angle was conducted. Fig. 2e delivers an image of a foam nickel after water drop without surface treatment, revealing a water contact angle of 103.9°. While it showcases an ultra-low water contact angle of merely 37.4° for nickel foam surface after loading B-CQDs/MnFeSe (Fig. 2f). The low water contact angle signifies that water can effectively permeate the surface, facilitating the interaction between the catalyst and the electrolyte. This enhances the adsorption of reactants, accelerates the electron transfer rate of the catalyst, and aids in more effectively evacuating products from active sites during the reaction process. Furthermore, favorable hydrophilia promotes the release of generated oxygen or hydrogen bubbles, enhances the catalytic efficiency of the catalyst, and showcases distinct catalytic performance.

To obtain a comprehensive insight into the surface chemical composition and electronic state of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF, XPS was employed to characterize the material. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, the acquired spectrum indicated the presence of Fe, Mn, O, C, B, and Se in the sample, which is consistent with the findings of EDS mapping [29]. Fig. 3b illustrates the presence of four convolution peaks in the high-resolution Mn 2p XPS spectrum of: Mn4+ at 655.1 and 644.2 eV, and Mn3+ at 653.6 and 641.1 eV [30]. These peaks are assigned to Mn2p3/2 and Mn2p1/2, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 3c, the characteristic peaks of Fe2+ and Fe3+ are observed at 709.7 and 721.0 eV, as well as 712.2 and 723.9 eV [31], which could be attributed to Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2, respectively. Fig. 3d displays the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Se 3d, featuring peaks of Se 3d5/2 and Se 3d3/2 at 55.3 eV and 58.1 eV [32], respectively. Additionally, in the high-resolution XPS of C 1s (Fig. 3e), the four peaks correspond to C–C (284.3 eV), C=C (284.8 eV), C–O (286.3 eV), and O–C=O (288.5 eV) [33], respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 3f, the faint characteristic peak in the high-resolution XPS spectrum of B 1s indicates that the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF composite contains a limited amount of B element. The result is highly consistent with the above characterization conclusions.

The composite catalyst with optimal catalytic performance is obtained by adjusting experimental conditions, such as the bimetallic ratio and carbon dot dosage, with the best conditions determined through performance testing, as shown in Fig. S4. The evaluation of the OER performance of the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF composite catalyst was conducted by a three-electrode system in a 1.0 M KOH aqueous solution at room temperature. As depicted in Fig. 4 a via linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF exhibits a notable advantage over B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF, MnFeO/NF, and Mn3O4. It is believed that the heterostructure of MnSe and FeSe creates more efficient charge transmission pathways, while the interface offers a wealth of active sites. Additionally, the incorporation of B-CQDs enhances electron transfer efficiency, thereby improving the catalytic performance of the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF composite. Fig. 4b illustrates a comparison of the corresponding overpotentials of the catalysts derived from the LSV curves (designated as η) at current densities of 10 mA cm−2 and 50 mA cm−2, respectively. The B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF exhibits the highest OER activity, possessing overpotentials of 197 mV and 281 mV at current densities of 10 mA cm−2 and 50 mA cm−2, respectively. These values are notably greater than those of B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF (η = 260 mV, 350 mV), MnFeO/NF (η = 325 mV, 453 mV), and Mn3O4 (η = 411 mV, 495 mV).

Additionally, the Tafel slope is regarded as an effective metric for estimating electrocatalytic behavior, thus, analyzing and fitting the LSV curves to derive Tafel slope curves, as shown in Fig. 4c. The slope of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF (74.61 mV dec−1) is significantly lower than that of B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF (92.21 mV dec−1), MnFeO/NF (107.65 mV dec−1), and Mn3O4 (194.87 mV dec−1), indicating that B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF exhibits faster OER reaction kinetics. It is consistent with the above LSV results. Based on the LSV curves, the turnover frequency (TOF) curves of the catalyst were obtained, as depicted in Fig. 4d. B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF demonstrates the maximum TOF value, potentially attributed to the special heterostructure, elevated density of active sites and superior adsorption/desorption kinetics. It is universally acknowledged that the conductivity and transport resistance are the primary determinants influencing the functionality of electrocatalysts in monolithic water splitting. Therefore, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed, as illustrated in Fig. 4e. The diameter of the semicircle in the Nyquist plot usually represents the charge transfer resistance (Rct), which is associated with the rate of the charge transfer process at the interface between the electrode material and the electrolyte. The smaller the diameter, the faster the charge transfer rate, and the better the catalyst performance. The Rct value of the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF catalyst is markedly lower than those of the B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF, MnFeO/NF, and Mn3O4 catalysts, which is in line with the minimum overpotential of the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF catalyst, signifying its low resistance, high catalytic efficiency, and rapid reaction rate. Additionally, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of the catalyst is directly proportional to the number of active sites. A higher ECSA value indicates that the catalyst has a significant number of active sites, which is more conducive to charge transfer and accordingly enhance the catalytic performance. Herein, ECSA was evaluated by deducing the double layer capacitance (Cdl) from the cyclic voltammetry (CV) at various scanning rates of 20–100 mV s−1 within the Faraday range. As depicted in Fig. 4f and Fig. S5, the Cdl of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF (13.75 mF cm−2) demonstrates a slight increase compared to B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF (12.37 mF cm−2), MnFeO/NF (9.37 mF cm−2), and Mn3O4 (6.59 mF cm−2). Furthermore, long-term durability is a crucial factor for the extensive utilization of high-efficiency electrocatalysts in practical production. To assess the HER stability of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF, the LSV curve was no significant alteration after 5000 CV scans compared to the initial curve (Fig. 4g). Fig. 4h represents the measurement of the timing potential (CP) for B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF under a fixed overpotential of 100 h at the current density of 10 mA cm−2, which only decreasing to 85.7% matching with the initial overpotential. These findings indicate that B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF demonstrates exceptional catalytic stability under prolonged alkaline electrolysis conditions.

The HER performance of these catalysts was evaluated under identical conditions to OER. It was observed that the overpotential of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF heterostructure (89 mV, 268 mV) was notably lower than that of B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF (η = 103 mV, 276 mV), MnFeO/NF (η = 273 mV, 442 mV), and Mn3O4 (η = 295 mV, 487 mV) at current densities of 10 mA cm−2 and 50 mA cm−2, respectively (Fig. 5 a and b). This implies that B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF serves as a more efficient catalyst, necessitating less energy to facilitate the HER reaction. The Tafel slope of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF exhibits a value of only 99.69 mV dec−1, indicating the HER process of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF primarily governed by the Volmer-Heyrovsky mechanism [17], showcasing specific heterostructure of MnSe and FeSe providing rapid catalytic rate and exceptional intrinsic activity (Fig. 5c). Additionally, B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF demonstrates the highest TOF value among all of catalysts, indicating its robust inherent electrocatalytic activity, which is consistent with LSV (Fig. 5d). The electrode kinetics of the catalysts were examined through EIS measurements.

As depicted in Fig. 5e, B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF demonstrated a lower charge transfer resistance compared to Mn3O4, MnFeO/NF, and B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF, meaning of more rapid chemical reaction kinetics. As illustrated in Fig. 5f and Fig. S6, B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF demonstrates the highest Cdl value (10.14 mF cm−2), outperforming B-CQDs/MnFeO/NF (9.51 mF cm−2), MnFeO/NF (5.38 mF cm−2), and Mn3O4 (3.85 mF cm−2), showcasing specific heterostructure interface of MnSe and FeSe providing more substantial number of active sites. Similarly, the LSV curves of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF prior to and following 5000 CV cycles were examined (Fig. 5g). The negligible difference between the polarization curve of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF and the original data is apparent. The long-term cycling performance of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF was evaluated at a current density at 10 mA cm−2 for 100 h (Fig. 5h), revealing that the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF remained stable, with only a slight reduction to 83.3% compared with the original overpotential. These findings further confirm the exceptional durability of B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF.

As a bifunctional catalyst, B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF demonstrates exceptional OER and HER capabilities. Consequently, B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF was employed as a self-assembled double electrode to evaluate its overall water decomposition performance. The experimental device describes the real-actual application of the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF catalyst within an electrolysis system in Fig. 6 a and 6a1-6a6 shows the schematic diagram of H2 and O2 produced every 10 min during overall water splitting process by drainage method. It is evident that the anode/cathode exhibits an O2 to H2 ratio of nearly 1:2, which aligns remarkably with the predicted outcome. Fig. 6b shows that the Faraday efficiency is close to 100%. As depicted in Fig. 6c, the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF||B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF configuration realized a current density of 10 mA cm−2 at 1.52 V merely, which is better than NF||NF (1.78 V). Furthermore, the 100-h cycling test demonstrated that the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF||B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF battery also exhibits excellent durability and stability (Fig. 6d). Therefore, it is conferred that B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF composite is indeed an efficient and stable monolithic hydrolysis electrocatalyst. In contrast to the manganese-based electrolysis cells documented in the recent literatures (Fig. 6e), this work demonstrates a superior application potential, thereby further substantiating the efficacy of fabricating heterostructures.

In summary, a novel strategy has been proposed in this work to enhance charge transfer and improve the hydrophilia for electrocatalyst via constructing MnSe and FeSe heterostructure. Herein, B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF displays high-performance bifunctional electrocatalysis by integrating the rich phase boundaries of heterostructure with hybrid of metal ions and empty d orbitals. Therefore, the B-CQDs/MnFeSe/NF self-supporting strategy only requires an overpotential of 89 mV and 197 mV to achieve a current density of 10 mA cm−2 in alkaline media, and enduring for 100 h only a slight reduction to 80.1%, surpassing most reported electrocatalysts recently. Overall, this work shows a fresh perspective for the design and manufacture of highly efficient dual-function electrocatalysts, demonstrating significant potential for the realization of an efficient and cost-effective water splitting technology.

Wenzhi Zhang: Writing – original draft. Mengmeng Guo: Data curation. Dong-Feng Chai: Writing – original draft. Guohua Dong: Writing – original draft. Liming Bai: Formal analysis. Zhuanfang Zhang: Software. Xinjia Zhang: Investigation. Dongxuan Guo: Software.
Data will be made available on request.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the NSF of China (32272823), Supported by Program for Young Talents of Basic Research in Universities of Heilongjiang Province (YQJH2023098), and Heilongjiang Province Young Scientific and Technological Talent Lift Project (2023QNTJ017).
